worth_it_explanation: PMP and PG deals are negotiated demand that a publisher has already lined up, so a stalled deal or a misaligned floor is unsold premium revenue rather than a missed open-auction bid. The check is a review of deal status and pricing inside Magnite Demand Manager and its reporting, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and each finding points to a specific deal ID the publisher can act on with the buyer.
intro: Programmatic deals route negotiated demand to a publisher through a Deal ID, a unique identifier carried in the bid request that lets a buyer's demand-side platform recognize the pre-agreed terms. Magnite handles two kinds: Private Marketplace (PMP) deals, which give a named buyer invitation-only access to inventory at an agreed floor, and Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deals, which commit a fixed volume at a fixed price. This subcategory is about the health of those deals once they are live and about how their floors and priorities sit against the open market, rather than the mechanics of negotiating them. These checks confirm that active deals are actually spending and that their pricing does not undercut the open auction or get undercut by it.

audit_checks.title: Active deal IDs are not monitored for spend and stalls
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A deal that is live in the system but not spending is negotiated demand the publisher set up and is now leaving unrealized. Without a regular read of spend by Deal ID, a stalled or near-dead deal can sit inactive for weeks while the publisher assumes the demand is flowing, so the revenue gap goes unseen until a buyer or a quarterly review surfaces it.

educate.base:
A programmatic deal in Magnite carries a Deal ID, a unique identifier that travels in the bid request so a buyer's demand-side platform (DSP) can recognize the pre-agreed terms and bid against them. A Private Marketplace (PMP) deal gives a named buyer invitation-only access at an agreed floor. A Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deal commits a set volume at a set price. In both cases the publisher has already done the work of lining up the demand, so a deal that stops spending is not a missed open-auction bid. It is negotiated revenue that the publisher arranged and is now leaving on the table.

Deals stall for reasons that are quiet from the publisher's side. A buyer can pause or repoint a campaign in their DSP, the targeting on the buyer's side can drift so the deal stops matching the publisher's inventory, a creative can fail to render and get throttled, or a deal can simply reach the end of its negotiated flight without anyone resetting it. None of these show up as an error in the publisher's account. The deal still appears active. The spend just goes to zero or trails off, and the only way to see it is to read spend by Deal ID over time.

Magnite's Demand Manager gives the publisher a centralized view of deals alongside other demand, and the platform's reporting can break revenue and impressions out by Deal ID. That makes deal health a measurable thing rather than an assumption. A publisher can list every active deal, read its recent spend, and flag any deal that has gone dark against what the buyer agreed to deliver.

A few signs point to deals running without health monitoring:
  • No regular report breaks spend and impressions out by Deal ID, so a stalled deal would not surface on its own
  • Deals appear in an active status long past their negotiated flight with no review of whether they are still delivering
  • The publisher cannot say which of its live deals are currently spending and which have gone quiet
You may want to confirm that active deals are reviewed for spend on a regular cadence. One approach is to pull a Deal ID report on a set schedule, compare each deal's recent spend against what the buyer committed to, and follow up on any deal that has stalled.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, PMP and PG deals are often tied to a specific section, a seasonal sponsorship, or a tentpole event, so a stalled deal can mean a buyer quietly walked away from premium inventory the publisher held for them. You may want to read spend by Deal ID against the flight dates of each sponsorship so a deal that goes dark mid-campaign is caught while there is still time to re-engage the buyer.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, deals may target product or category pages where the audience is closer to a purchase and the agreed price reflects that intent. You may want to monitor spend on those higher-value deals specifically since a stall there leaves the most valuable inventory unsold rather than the general editorial pages.

educate.learn_more_note: Magnite's detailed walkthroughs for reading deal spend and managing deal status in Demand Manager are in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: List the active deal IDs and read recent spend and impressions for each to see which deals are delivering and which have stalled.

magnite_demand_manager.label: Magnite Demand Manager
steps: Open Demand Manager and list the active deals with their Deal IDs, named buyers, and negotiated flight dates.
For each deal, note its status and whether it is within its agreed flight or has passed the end date without being reset.
Record which deals the publisher expects to be spending right now and which were set up for a flight that has ended.

magnite_reporting.label: Magnite reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by Deal ID with revenue, impressions, and recent spend over a trailing window.
Compare each deal's recent spend against the volume or pace the buyer committed to in the negotiation.
Flag any deal that shows zero or near-zero spend while still marked active, and any deal trailing well below its committed delivery.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per active Deal ID, its status, its negotiated flight, and its recent spend against what the buyer committed to deliver. A set of deals where each active deal is spending in line with its agreement, or has been closed out when its flight ended, passes. A setup where active deals show zero or near-zero spend with no review, or deals sit active past their flight, is a fail recorded with the specific Deal IDs that have stalled.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the status and recent spend for each active Deal ID above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs active programmatic deals without monitoring spend by Deal ID, so a stalled deal can sit inactive while the demand is assumed to be flowing. A Private Marketplace (PMP) or Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deal that stops spending is negotiated revenue the publisher arranged and is now leaving unrealized, and deals stall quietly because a buyer pauses or repoints a campaign in their demand-side platform (DSP) without any error showing on the publisher's side. {{voice_recommend}} pulling a Deal ID report on a set cadence, comparing each deal's recent spend against what the buyer committed to deliver, and following up on any deal that has gone dark. After that, keep the report on a regular schedule so a stall surfaces while there is still time to re-engage the buyer.

audit_checks.title: Deal floors and priorities are not aligned with the open market
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A deal floor set below the open-market clearing price lets a buyer win agreed inventory for less than the open auction would have paid, and a deal that does not get priority over the open market can be outbid on the inventory it was meant to secure. Either way the pricing and priority do not reflect the deal's intent, so the publisher trades away value on inventory it negotiated to protect.

educate.base:
A programmatic deal carries its own floor, the minimum price the buyer agreed to pay, and its own priority, which decides whether the deal gets first claim on an impression or competes alongside the open auction. Both settings have to fit the deal's intent. A Private Marketplace (PMP) deal exists to give a named buyer preferred access at an agreed price, and a Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deal commits a fixed volume at a fixed price. If the floor or the priority drifts away from that intent, the deal stops doing what it was set up to do.

Two failure modes show up. The first is a deal floor that sits below what the open market is already paying for the same inventory. When that happens, the deal lets the buyer win impressions for less than the open auction would have cleared, so the publisher hands a discount to a buyer who was prepared to pay the market rate. The second is a deal that does not hold priority over the open auction on the inventory it was meant to secure. A PG deal in particular represents a commitment, so it should win its agreed impressions rather than lose them to a higher open-market bid, and a PMP deal's preferred access only means something if the deal is positioned to win.

The audit question is whether each deal's floor and priority line up with the open-market price and with the deal's purpose. Magnite Demand Manager carries the floor and priority settings, and the reporting shows the open-market clearing price for the same inventory, so the comparison is a matter of reading both side by side. The IAB Tech Lab OpenRTB specification defines the Deal and private marketplace objects that carry a deal's floor through the bid request, which is the same plumbing every deal runs on.

A few signs point to deal floors and priorities that are out of line:
  • A deal floor is at or below the average open-market clearing price for the same ad units, so the deal undercuts the open auction
  • A Programmatic Guaranteed deal does not hold priority over the open market and loses its committed impressions to higher open bids
  • The publisher cannot say how each deal's floor compares to the current open-market price for that inventory
You may want to confirm that each deal's floor sits at or above the open-market price for the same inventory and that the priority matches the deal's commitment. One approach is to read the open-market clearing price per ad unit, compare it against each deal's floor, and adjust the floors or priorities that do not fit.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a PMP deal on a premium section is meant to give a named buyer access at a price that protects the section's value, so a floor that sits below the open-market rate for that section gives the value away. You may want to compare each deal's floor against the open-market clearing price for the same placement rather than a site-wide average since the premium sections are where a low deal floor costs the most.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, deals on high-intent product and category pages should hold floors that reflect how those pages clear, which is usually above the editorial pages. You may want to check that a deal targeting commercial inventory carries a floor matched to that inventory's open-market price, so the deal does not let a buyer win high-intent impressions at an editorial-page rate.

educate.learn_more_note: Magnite's detailed configuration guides for deal floors and priority in Demand Manager are in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review each deal's floor and priority and compare them against the open-market clearing price for the same inventory.

magnite_demand_manager.label: Magnite Demand Manager
steps: Open Demand Manager and list the active deals with their Deal IDs, agreed floors, deal type (PMP or PG), and priority settings.
For each deal, note the inventory it targets and whether it is set to hold priority over the open auction or to compete alongside it.
Record any deal whose priority does not match its type, such as a Programmatic Guaranteed deal that is not positioned to win its committed impressions.

magnite_reporting.label: Magnite reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by ad unit with the average open-market clearing price, restricted to the inventory each deal targets.
Compare each deal's agreed floor against the open-market clearing price for the same inventory.
Flag any deal whose floor sits at or below the open-market price, and any Programmatic Guaranteed deal losing committed impressions to higher open bids.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per Deal ID, the agreed floor, the deal type, the priority setting, and the open-market clearing price for the same inventory. Deals whose floors sit at or above the open-market price and whose priority matches the deal's commitment pass. A deal floor at or below the open-market price, or a Programmatic Guaranteed deal that does not hold priority over the open auction, is a fail recorded with the Deal ID and the gap between the floor and the open-market price.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each deal's floor, priority, and the open-market clearing price for the same inventory above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} has programmatic deals whose floors and priorities are not aligned with the open market. A deal floor below the open-market clearing price lets a buyer win agreed inventory for less than the open auction would have paid, and a Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deal that does not hold priority over the open market can lose its committed impressions to higher open bids, so the pricing and priority stop matching the deal's intent. {{voice_recommend}} reading the open-market clearing price per ad unit, comparing it against each deal's floor in Magnite Demand Manager, and adjusting the floors and priorities that do not fit the inventory or the deal's commitment. After the change, recheck each deal's delivery and clearing price to confirm the floors hold and the committed deals win their impressions.
